variables.content: The measured quasiparticle effective mass m* of two-dimensional electrons confined in a silicon (100) inversion layer is described, over the experimentally accessible interaction-parameter interval r_s ≈ 1.7 to 3.3, by the empirical linear relation m*/m_b = 0.96 + γ r_s, where m_b = 0.19 m_e is the bare in-plane band mass, m_e is the free electron mass, r_s is the two-dimensional interaction parameter defined by r_s = 1/(a_B sqrt(π n_s)) with a_B the semiconductor Bohr radius and n_s the sheet electron density, and γ is a dimensionless prefactor that parameterizes the rate of increase of the normalized effective mass with r_s.

Define the two-dimensional interaction parameter r_s for a two-dimensional hole system of sheet density p (units cm^-2) by r_s ≡ E_{e-e}/E_F, where the characteristic electron–electron interaction energy is taken as E_{e-e} = (e^2/4π ε_r ε_0) √(π p) and the Fermi energy for two-dimensional holes is taken as E_F = π ħ^2 p/(2 m_h^*), with ε_r the material relative permittivity, ε_0 the vacuum permittivity, ħ the reduced Planck constant, and m_h^* the two-dimensional hole effective mass. The proposition is: numerically estimating r_s from these formulas to obtain r_s ≈ 10 at p = 2.5×10^13 cm^-2 requires selecting specific numerical values for ε_r and m_h^* that are uncertain for the rubrene surface in the experimental gating geometry (the ionic-liquid environment and surface bandstructure can alter dielectric screening and effective mass); therefore the numerical statement "r_s ≈ 10" is contingent on these parameter choices and carries uncertainty proportional to the uncertainties in ε_r and m_h^*.

Define the two-dimensional interaction parameter r_{s} by r_{s} = \dfrac{\displaystyle \frac{m e^{2}}{4\pi\hbar^{2}\kappa\varepsilon_{0}}}{\displaystyle (\pi n)^{1/2}}, where m is the electron band effective mass (in units of the free electron mass), e is the elementary charge, ħ is the reduced Planck constant, κ is the material dielectric constant, ε_{0} is vacuum permittivity, and n is the two-dimensional electron density in units of cm^{-2}. For m = 0.46 and κ = 10, substituting n = n_{V} ≃ 6.3×10^{10} cm^{-2} into the formula yields r_{s} of order twenty (r_{s} ≃ 20); this statement assumes the use of the isotropic in-plane effective mass average and neglects small corrections from anisotropy in the definition of r_{s}.

The two-dimensional electron-gas interaction parameter $r_s$ for areal density $n_{\rm s}$ is defined as $r_s = 1/\bigl(\sqrt{\pi n_{\rm s}}\,a_{\rm B}^*\bigr)$ where the effective Bohr radius is $a_{\rm B}^* = a_{\rm B}\,\epsilon_r\,m_0/m^*$ with $a_{\rm B}$ the vacuum Bohr radius, $\epsilon_r$ the relative dielectric constant of GaAs, $m_0$ the free-electron mass and $m^*$ the GaAs effective mass; inserting $n_{\rm s}=4.8\times10^{15}\ \text{m}^{-2}$ and standard GaAs material parameters for $\epsilon_r$ and $m^*$ yields $r_s\approx0.8$, which serves as an appropriate measure of the relative interaction strength of electrons in the heterostructure two-dimensional electron gas for low-order interaction estimates.

Define the dimensionless interaction parameter r_s for a two-dimensional carrier system as r_s = a/a_B^*, where the average intercarrier spacing a = 1/√(π p) for two-dimensional density p and the effective Bohr radius a_B^* = 4πϵ ħ^2/(m^* e^2) depends inversely on the effective mass m^*; the proposition is that computing r_s using a fixed, unrenormalized effective mass m^* = 0.2 m_e (with m_e the free-electron mass) yields the r_s estimates used to compare regimes across materials in this study, but these r_s estimates may be underestimated if interactions renormalize m^* upward in the dilute GaAs two-dimensional hole system.
      B. E. Cole et al., Phys. Rev. B 55, 2503 (1997).

In a two-dimensional electron Fermi liquid realized in GaAs/AlGaAs quantum wells, the density-of-states effective mass m_{e}^{\mathrm{FL}} defined by m_{e}^{\mathrm{FL}}=\dfrac{\pi\hbar^{2} n_{S}}{E_{F}} (where n_{S} is the two-dimensional electron concentration, E_{F} is the spectroscopic Fermi energy measured from the size-quantization band bottom, \hbar is Planck’s constant divided by 2\pi, and all quantities refer to a single occupied subband) increases as the electron density decreases; equivalently, m_{e}^{\mathrm{FL}} increases with the interaction parameter r_{S}=1/\bigl[a_{B}(\pi n_{S})^{1/2}\bigr] (where a_{B} is the Bohr radius). Quantitatively, when r_{S} is increased from about 1 to about 4.5 the measured m_{e}^{\mathrm{FL}} increases by approximately 35% relative to the band cyclotron mass in GaAs (the standard GaAs band cyclotron mass used for comparison is m_{e}=0.067\,m_{0}, where m_{0} is the free-electron mass).

The exchange interaction among itinerant electrons in the two-dimensional electron gas is represented at the mean-field level by an isotropic effective precession frequency contribution
      $$
      \boldsymbol{\omega}_{ee}(\mathbf{r},t)=-2G\,n_{e}\,\nu^{-1}\mathbf{s}_{0}(\mathbf{r},t),
      $$
      where $G$ is the dimensionless electron–electron interaction constant (taken to satisfy $G<0$ and $|G|<1$), $n_{e}$ is the electron two-dimensional concentration, $\nu=m/2\pi\hbar^{2}$ is the two-dimensional density of states for electron effective mass $m$, and $\mathbf{s}_{0}(\mathbf{r},t)$ is the local averaged electron spin density. This isotropic, local approximation neglects momentum-dependent, anisotropic, retardation, and correlation corrections to electron–electron exchange beyond the simple Fermi-liquid-like parametrization by the single number $G$.

Over the experimentally accessed two-dimensional interaction-parameter interval r_s = 1/(a_B sqrt(π n_s)) with r_s ∈ [1.7, 3.3] (where a_B is the semiconductor Bohr radius and n_s is the sheet density expressed in cm^−2), the dependence of the renormalized effective mass on r_s is accurately represented, within the experimental uncertainties of the reported data, by the single-linear-parameter empirical model m*/m_b = 0.96 + γ r_s, where m_b = 0.19 m_e.

For a two‑dimensional electron system in a MgZnO/ZnO heterojunction the dimensionless interparticle spacing is $r_s=(\pi n_s)^{-1/2}/a_{\rm B}$, where $n_s$ is the areal electron density and $a_{\rm B}=\epsilon\hbar^2/(me^2)$ is the effective Bohr radius for ZnO with dielectric constant $\epsilon$ and electron effective mass $m$; at the experimentally relevant value $r_s\approx 6.4$ the electronic ground state is a triangular Wigner crystal (possibly a glassy Wigner state) because calculations including impurity effects indicate that disorder shifts the liquid–crystal transition from the clean‑limit value $r_s\approx 37$ down to approximately $r_s\approx 7$, making crystallization plausible at $r_s\approx 6.4$.
      [5]
      [6]

The spin-density static structure factor \tilde{S}(q) was computed self-consistently within the QSTLS formalism for two geometries: (i) a strictly two-dimensional (2D) electron liquid obtained by setting the form factor F(q)=1 in the effective interaction V(q), and (ii) a quasi-two-dimensional (Q2D) electron liquid in which F(q) is the finite-thickness form factor obtained from Bastard's variational envelope-function treatment of the confinement normal to the plane; calculations were performed at zero temperature for the dimensionless density parameters r_{s}=1,2,3,4 (with r_{s}=1/\sqrt{\pi n a_{B}^{2}} and a_{B} the effective Bohr radius appropriate to the averaged dielectric constant and chosen effective mass), and the Q2D \tilde{S}(q) shows a significant modification across a broad range of q relative to the strictly 2D \tilde{S}(q) for these densities.
